posts.name: BILL D A V I E S
posts.cooked:
I love Chardonnay, but my wallet can’t keep up with my palate. I’ve been exploring alternatives like mineral driven white wines from Ischia, high elevation Chardonnay from Argentina, floral rich dry Grenache Blanc from Spain… but nothing quite does it like Chablis for me.

I’ve found a happy place with a lot of South African wines, but always looking to try new things.

Would love the help/recommendations for others Chardonnay lovers. A part-time helper is back for the summer and I also want to share some wine with them before they go back to law school.
posts.display_username: BILL D A V I E S

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked: IMO Chablis isn’t really an alternative to Raveneau, because stylistically Raveneau is pretty unique. I find their wines more similar to the whites of Côte de Beaune rather than being particularly typical of contemporary Chablis. If you want to drink Raveneau, drinking some other producer’s Chablis doesn’t really scratch the itch.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Robert_Dentice:

With that said I would recommend trying German Silvaner and Chardonnay.

This is a pretty broad-sweeping statement. I doubt a Horst Sauer Silvaner will work as an alternative for a Raveneau, no matter how great the wine is. If you have some specific labels in mind, please do tell.

As for German wines, the Weissburgunders by Friedrich Becker are stylistically made in a relatively similar style to Raveneau’s wines. They might not be Chardonnay, but they hit the same spot as Raveneau - and at more reasonable prices. At just 20€ Kalkgestein Weissburgunder is a screaming bargain.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Vorberg, not Vorbourg.

It’s definitely a fantastic wine that can age gracefully for decades. However, I myself don’t find anything in common with Vorberg and Raveneau. Raveneau is mineral, precise and shows some oak influence. Vorberg is a rich, a lot more textural wine with tons of more fruit, less minerality and no obvious oak influence. I really don’t see how Vorberg could be an alternative to Raveneau - unless people here are just randomly throwing names of great white wines, no matter if they are in any way similar to Raveneau (I don’t know about you guys, but if I myself was asking an alternative to Raveneau, I’d want to hear about wines that feel and taste similar to Raveneau).

posts.cooked:

I’ve never had Raveneau but what I find amusing is that if it was anything but Raveneau, it would be criticized for being unlike what Chablis is supposed to be.

I don’t know. Some people love Pattes Loup wines like crazy even if they are quite atypical of Chablis.

posts.name: Eric Egan
posts.cooked:
Difficult to find a substitute for Raveneau! But if I felt the need to scratch the itch without any available I guess I might do worse than a bottle of Jülg Opus-Oskar (chardonnay from Pfalz). There are a bunch of other German wines that also might hit the mark stylistically (well, not quite of course), but that would be a good place to start. While I agree that you could look towards the Cote de Beaune, I think it’s difficult to find anything there with the same type of core and poise - packing a serious punch with the same degree of subtlety.

Of course Keller’s chardonnays also have some things in common with Raveneau stylistically but they’re probably significanty harder to source.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

Or perhaps Raveneau has become so big precisely because it tastes nothing like traditional Chablis and people like it better.

Nobody said Raveneau tasted nothing like traditional Chablis, on the contrary. To my understanding the style Raveneau represents is more similar to the traditional style of Chablis, which has slowly disappeared in the wake of modern winemaking (and stainless steel tanks). The only difference is that traditionally the Chablis producers have employed older and/or larger oak barrels, while the Raveneau style has relied on more noticeable oak impact.

And I don’t think Raveneau has become so big because of this. It was big even back when the lean, clean and mineral style of contemporary Chablis wasn’t the norm.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
Hello Otto,

I haven’t had as much Raveneau as I would like, but have reasons for the comparison with Vorberg.

I find Raveneau significantly richer than most Chablis, I could say more textural. I also find significant minerality in Vorberg, more than in most Chablis.

No, it’s not really a close match, but what else is? I think that somebody who appreciates Raveneau might find Vorberg enjoyable both for some similarities and as a counterpart.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
This is a pretty interesting exchange, which throws into relief the challenges of using terms such as “traditional” and “modern” with reference to ultimately arbitrary waypoints on an evolutionary continuum. Raveneau is a “traditionalist” in a lot of ways, but also modernized quite a bit with Bernard Raveneau, who returned to the family domaine after launching the Vézelay coop in the mid-1980s. The wines ferment in vat and are racked quite clean to barrels (whereas Dauvissat rack quite turbid), which are renewed reasonably frequently while remaining predominantly used, and which include some larger format wood for some cuvées. Doses of SO2 were pretty high under Bernard and seem to be a bit lower these days… I have no idea what they were like before 1985, as the old wines are too good and too rare for me to have ever sent a sample to the lab. Dauvissat today is for me more “traditional”, as they still use some feuillettes (the traditional barrel size in Chablis, and the unit by which must/bulk wine is still sold) for the cuvées from more marly, reductive terroirs; they retain more lees; and they use less sulfites; they also renew their barrels a touch less frequently (at least, that’s my impression).

For a lot of people today, however, “traditional” Chablis goes back to the 1970s and means fermentation in stainless steel followed by bottling quite early… And this exists in contradistinction to “Modern” Chablis which sees an overt new oak influence.

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

This is a pretty interesting exchange, which throws into relief the challenges of using terms such as “traditional” and “modern” with reference to ultimately arbitrary waypoints on an evolutionary continuum.

Agreed, William. While many of us, myself included, use the “traditional” term often, I think we’re usually referring to wines made in the style before the big 1990s international style/lots of new wood/Parker era. But when you think about it, going further back in time I’m guessing that Chablis from the 1800s was probably richer, sweeter and very different than what we might expect today. I’ve never tasted a Chablis anywhere near that old, but maybe you have?

The question I have always wondered regarding Raveneau is why are they so different? I was lucky enough to taste at/with Raveneau a few times on the Kermit Lynch buying trip years ago and when I asked that question Bernard’s response was that they attributed the difference to their intense work in the vineyard and lower yields than most producers. I would love to hear your take on it.

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked:
One component that contributes to the Raveneau style that is seldom discussed is that Raveneau to my knowledge still does not use native yeast. They inoculate.

There is a great interview with Isabelle that I can’t seem to find where she explains that this is a common misconception, and that they have always and still do inoculate to start fermentation (I believe is a Vinous article but my subscription has expired). It’s funny because you will often see offers for Raveneau from various retailers claiming they use native yeast.

posts.name: Matthew Brown
posts.cooked: If this is on your radar as a possibility, vineyard sites in the Santa Cruz Mountains in general hold good potential. Mount Eden is probably the apex (I would put them pound for pound and dollar for dollar as one of the best California Chardonnay options, full stop) but single site bottlings tend to get combinations of both higher acidity and natural intensity. Oak will vary from producer to producer, but most that I’ve seen tend to be on the less aggressive side.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Raveneau is a “traditionalist” in a lot of ways, but also modernized quite a bit with Bernard Raveneau, who returned to the family domaine after launching the Vézelay coop in the mid-1980s. The wines ferment in vat and are racked quite clean to barrels (whereas Dauvissat rack quite turbid), which are renewed reasonably frequently while remaining predominantly used, and which include some larger format wood for some cuvées. Doses of SO2 were pretty high under Bernard and seem to be a bit lower these days… I have no idea what they were like before 1985, as the old wines are too good and too rare for me to have ever sent a sample to the lab. Dauvissat today is for me more “traditional”, as they still use some feuillettes (the traditional barrel size in Chablis, and the unit by which must/bulk wine is still sold) for the cuvées from more marly, reductive terroirs; they retain more lees; and they use less sulfites; they also renew their barrels a touch less frequently (at least, that’s my impression).

This goes some way in explaining why I, notwithstanding specific bottles, have come to prefer Dauvissat over Raveneau. There is often more of the saline and tidepool note in Dauvissat wines that I find less often in Raveneau.

posts.name: John Vizuete
posts.cooked:
Within Chablis, the Moreau Naudet wines come as close to scratching the Raveneau itch for me at 1/5th or 6th the price. Admittedly not the same thing, but occasionally a bottle like the 2017 Montmains will pull it off. I seem to prefer Raveneau in sunnier years (I know this puts me in the minority) because it further accentuates the exotic, sometimes tropical style, which Moreau-Naudet sometimes hits.

I like the Pico wines as well and understand what Ian is alluding to with regard to texture, but all the Pattes Loup I’ve had comes across a bit leesy in comparison.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
J.Vizuete:

I like the Pico wines as well and understand what Ian is alluding to with regard to texture, but all the Pattes Loup I’ve had comes across a bit leesy in comparison.

Better said than I. I think if they picked a little sooner, and did the whole lees aging thing, it would probably work out better.

Trying to find an alternative to Raveneau (to me) is like trying to find an alternative to Raul Perez’s Sketch, or Keller’s G-Max. Some wines are just so singular that there is no “alternative”.

I would suggest you focus on trying to find other singular wines that are out there and are not yet impossible to secure.
Some producers that are still available (and special in my mind):
Virgen del Galir
Wittmann
Goodfellow
Guiberteau
Tement
Seehof
Gérard Boulay

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: When the vast majority of a region is machine harvested, enzymed, fermented and tank for bottling after sterile filtration within a year, the fact of picking by hand, maturing in barrel and doing two winters’ élevage will already open up a huge delta. Moderate yields when compared with the norm and plenty of old vines will only compound that.

posts.name: John Vizuete

I’ve tasted at Ceritas and have had maybe a dozen of their whites. These tend to be early picked, acid driven, and laser focused by California standards. They see minimal if any new oak.

I like them and really appreciate what John Raytek and his team are trying to achieve, but would be one of the last Chardonnays to come to my mind when looking for an alternative to Raveneau, where nearly every factor from yeasts to barrels is in stark contrast.

posts.cooked: since this thread is drifted, i don’t feel too badly; dauvissat vs raveneau - which has a better record on premox?

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
ybarselah:

since this thread is drifted, i don’t feel too badly; dauvissat vs raveneau - which has a better record on premox?

From my experience Raveneau has the much better track record and it’s not even close. I’m guessing 8X higher incidence of premox with Dauvissat.

I’m updating the post with my CT stats, 7X higher is the actual comparison.

Premox rates:

Raveneau 6 out of 239 (2.1%)
Dauvissat 19 out of 128 (14.8%)

I got fed up and sent 188 bottles of Dauvissat off to auction.

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked:
I’ve opened a number of Dauvissat bottles in the last 8 months at a variety of ages and have not had any issues with premox. This has led me to consider what may have contributed to all of the well documented premox issues that many Berserkers have discussed here (present company included). Clearly some of the wines below are a little young, but given that premox typically begins to rear it’s ugly head at 5 – 7 years old I think most of these have some level of relevance.

Wines I have opened in the 8 Months

  • 1 x 2012 Les Clos
  • 1 x 2009 Les Clos
  • 1 x 2019 La Forest
  • 1 x 2017 La Forest
  • 1 x 2014 La Forest
  • 1 x 2012 La Forest
  • 1 x 2010 La Forest
  • 1 x 2016 Vaillons
  • 2 x 2020 Village Chablis
  • 2 x 2018 Village Chablis
  • 2 x 2017 Village Chablis

The one thread that all of these bottles have in common is that they were purchased directly from France at auction within the last 2 years (from several different lots). Something I have noticed on many bottles of Dauvissat that I have opened is there are often clear signs that the bottles have been overfilled. Essentially there is dried oxidized wine between the capsule and the cork (often caked to top of the cork). For bottles with a wax capsule the green French customs seal was often stained, and in rare cases completely saturated with a small quantity of wine that had ooze through a bubble in the wax capsule. The wines that displayed this also tend to have the highest fill levels. All of these bottles were pristine. My theory is this: had these wines been shipped while the corks were still slippery and saturated with wine they would have been much more susceptible to moving up and down the neck of the bottle and letting oxygen in with the typical temperature fluctuations that wines see in transit to retail shelves here in the USA. Traveling a short distances to a cellar in France where they are allowed to rest for a number of years on the other hand, and then shipping to the US via air directly to a temp controlled wine storage facility has led to better results. This would also explain why Vincent has never been able to identify premox issues in his own cellar.

Regardless, I love the wines and will continue to take the risk. Here’s hoping the issue is largely behind the Domaine.

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
Larry_Link:
ybarselah:

since this thread is drifted, i don’t feel too badly; dauvissat vs raveneau - which has a better record on premox?

From my experience Raveneau has the much better track record and it’s not even close. I’m guessing 8X higher incidence of premox with Dauvissat.

I’m updating the post with my CT stats, 7X higher is the actual comparison.

Premox rates:

Raveneau 6 out of 239 (2.1%)
Dauvissat 19 out of 128 (14.8%)

I got fed up and sent 188 bottles of Dauvissat off to auction.

I just spent some more time digging, as 6 bottles of premoxed Raveneau seemed too high. In looking at those 6 bottles, 3 were actually corked and 3 were premoxed. So my premox level of Raveneau is actually

3 out of 239 (1.2%)

As an aside, it would be great if CellarTracker separated the Spoiled (Corked/Oxidized) consumption type field into 2 separate categories/fields of “Spoiled Corked” and “Spoiled Premoxed” to facilitate keeping track of the failures.

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
ybarselah:

you need a multi-select / checkbox drop down for wine flaws. like adding toppings to a pizza.

Are you a CT user? You can pick from a list (drank, brought to a tasting, missing etc.), and for spoiled wines your options are:

Spoiled (corked/oxidized) and returned to vendor
Spoiled (corked/oxidized) but not returned

I add either corked or oxidized in a consumption note to differentiate, but when you pull reports they get lumped together.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
replacement bottle was correct. took a very long time to open up and still pours slightly darker than what i’d expect of a very young chablis - some actual yellow color as opposed to mostly clear / lime. what stands out most is that it comes off a bit as a … natural wine. wild aromatics and high acidity. tons of persistence with dry extract and acidity. perhaps this lot wasn’t to par or the vintage or a style i’m not totally used to. difficult to pin down. had i tried it blind, i’m not sure i’d go to chablis right away. the last glass was the best and i wanted more - perhaps the best and true test.

now i need to dig deeper into this producer…

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
B_Davies:

Would love the help/recommendations for others Chardonnay lovers.

Bill, Domaine Lavantureux may be an option. Kermit Lynch has imported the wines for many years, first those made by father Roland, and now those made by his son, Arnaud (with help from his brother). Arnaud maintains that he favors a Cote de Beaune approach to winemaking; he likes the nervous, chiseled, mineral aspect conferred by stainless steel tanks, but also the structure and complexity contributed by barrel aging. He uses a high proportion of old barrels for his 1er crus and grand crus. I find the domaine’s village lieu-dit Vauprin, 1er cru Vau de Vey, and grand cru Vaudesir to be particularly compelling.

https://chablis-lavantureux.fr/en/

https://kermitlynch.com/grower/domaine-roland-lavantureux

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: More so in Chablis than CdB (the later you harvest, the more likely you are to get botrytis, all things being equal, and Chablis harvests after the CdB).
